seoTitle: How to Create Card Button Automations in Trello
metaDescription: Build a custom Card Button in Trello's Butler engine that runs a multi-step macro, like assigning, labeling, and moving a card in one click.
intro: Manually assigning yourself, adding a label, and moving a card across several menus adds up over a busy day. This guide covers building a custom Card Button that compresses that whole sequence into a single click.

steps.heading: Open the Automation workspace
steps.body: From your board layout options, open the central Automation dashboard.

steps.heading: Start a new Card Button
steps.body: Select the option to create a custom button that will live on the back of your cards.

steps.heading: Design the button interface
steps.body: Choose an icon and write a clear, descriptive label and title for the button.

steps.heading: Stack your actions
steps.body: Add multiple steps to the button, such as moving the card, applying a color label, and appending a checklist.

steps.heading: Test the button
steps.body: Click the button on a sample card to confirm each action runs correctly.

steps.heading: Fix common issues
steps.body: Check for broken member assignments or incorrect destination rules if the button doesn't behave as expected.

faq.question: What's the difference between a Card Button and a Board Rule?
faq.answer: A Card Button runs manually when clicked, while a Board Rule triggers automatically based on a board event.

faq.question: How many actions can one button perform?
faq.answer: You can stack multiple actions into a single button, such as moving lists, adding tags, and appending checklist steps together.

faq.question: Where does the button appear?
faq.answer: Once created, the custom button lives right on the back of your Trello cards for easy access.

faq.question: What should I check if my button isn't working correctly?
faq.answer: Look for broken member assignments or incorrect destination rules, which are common causes of action sequence failures.
